content: The alarming rise of antibiotic resistance is largely attributed to the confluence of overprescription, agricultural misuse, and horizontal gene transfer. Overprescription in human medicine has accelerated the selective pressure driving bacterial evolution towards resistance, while the widespread use of antibiotics in agriculture has further exacerbated the issue. Horizontal gene transfer, facilitated by mobile genetic elements, enables the rapid dissemination of resistance genes among bacterial populations. This perfect storm has resulted in the emergence of highly resistant strains, such as carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae. The consequences are dire, with the World Health Organization warning of a post-antibiotic era. As the pipeline for new antibiotics dwindles, it is imperative that we adopt a multifaceted approach to mitigate the spread of resistance, including stringent stewardship, innovative therapeutic strategies, and enhanced surveillance. The clock is ticking, and concerted action is required to avert a global health crisis.
